1: rabbat ṣĕrārûnî minnĕʿûray yōmar-nāʾ yiśrāʾēl — "many times they have afflicted me from my youth — let Israel say this now" — rabbat (many times, much, greatly) — the same opening word as Psalms 123:4 (exceedingly filled). The liturgical call yōmar-nāʾ yiśrāʾēl (let Israel now say) echoes Psalms 124:1 — this is Israel's communal testimony, not merely one individual's experience. minnĕʿûray (from my youth) — Israel personified as a nation from its origins (Egypt); the affliction has been lifelong, going back to the bondage in Egypt.
2: rabbat ṣĕrārûnî minnĕʿûray gam lōʾ-yāklû lî — "many times they have afflicted me from my youth, and yet they have never overcome me" — the verbatim repetition of the first half-line followed by the crucial gam lōʾ-yāklû lî (yet they have not prevailed against me) — the adversative gam (and yet, even, also). The repeated oppression combined with the repeated non-defeat is the theological testimony: enemies are persistent, but YHWH is more persistent. yākol (to prevail, be able, overcome) — the enemies' inability is the sign of divine protection.
3: ʿal-gabbî ḥārĕšû ḥōrĕšîm hē ʾărîkû lĕmaʿănîtôtām — "they plowed across my back; they made long their furrows" — ʿal-gabbî (upon my back) — the metaphor of the plowed back is one of the Psalter's most vivid images of suffering; the back being plowed evokes flogging, where the stripes of the whip cut furrows in the flesh. hē ʾărîkû lĕmaʿănîtôtām (they made long their furrows) — extended, deep, deliberate furrows. The agricultural metaphor frames brutal violence in terms of cultivation — the oppressors turn Israel's back into their field, treating the people as the ground for their own purposes.
4: yhwh ṣaddîq qiṣṣēṣ ʿăbôt rĕšāʿîm — "the LORD is righteous; he has cut through the ropes of the wicked" — ṣaddîq (righteous) — YHWH's righteousness here is his active justice on behalf of the wronged. qiṣṣēṣ (he cut, severed, Piel of qāṣaṣ) — the cutting is decisive and complete. ʿăbôt rĕšāʿîm (the cords/ropes of the wicked) — the ropes by which the wicked bound Israel, binding them to the plow (cf. v3's furrow). The image now inverts: the plowed field becomes the bound ox, and YHWH cuts the yoke-cords.
5: yēbōšû wĕyissōgû ʾāḥôr kōl śōnĕʾê ṣiyyôn — "may every one who hates Zion be put to shame and driven back" — the psalm shifts from testimony (vv1-4) to imprecation (vv5-8). yēbōšû (may they be ashamed, Qal jussive of bôš) — shame is the social opposite of honor in the ancient Near Eastern world; to be shamed is to be publicly exposed as having no standing. yissōgû ʾāḥôr (may they be driven back, retreating backwards) — the military image of an army in rout, retreating without turning to fight.
6: yihyû kĕḥăṣîr gakkôt šeqĕdem šālap yābeš — "may they be like grass growing on a flat rooftop, which withers before it even has a chance to grow" — ḥăṣîr gakkôt (the grass of rooftops/flat roofs) — the flat mud-brick roofs of ancient Palestine had cracks into which seeds blew; grass would sprout briefly in the rainy season but wither almost immediately in the dry season — no deep roots, no shade, no harvest. šeqĕdem šālap yābeš (which before it is drawn out, it withers) — before it even reaches maturity, before anyone could think of harvesting it, it shrivels. The image answers the plowing of v3: their "harvest" from Israel's back will produce nothing.
7: šellōʾ millēʾ kappô qôṣēr wĕḥiṣnô mĕʿammēr — "no reaper fills his hand with it, and no one gathers it into sheaves" — the harvest imagery completes the agricultural metaphor: the rooftop-grass gives no handful to the reaper (qôṣēr) and no armful (ḥōṣen) to the one binding sheaves (mĕʿammēr). The wicked who plowed Israel's back hoping for a harvest will be like this futile rooftop grass — their effort produces absolutely nothing worth gathering.
8: wĕlōʾ ʾāmĕrû hāʾōbĕrîm birkăt-yhwh ʾălêkem bērăknû ʾetkem bĕšēm yhwh — "and no one passing by will say, 'The LORD's blessing be on you! We bless you in the name of the LORD'" — the standard blessing exchanged between reapers and those passing by during harvest (Ruth 2:4: Boaz greets the reapers: "The LORD be with you!" and they reply: "The LORD bless you!"). The absence of this exchange signals total failure: there is nothing to bless, no harvest to celebrate, no encounter of generosity between the field and the passerby. The wicked will not even receive the customary pleasantries of a good harvest — because there is none.
